sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After clicking on the purple square (node 24 at row 57-60, col 26-29), I can see from the image that the purple square now has a white border around it, indicating it's selected. The timer bar (gray line) likely decreased again.
So the game mechanics seem to be:
1. SPACE shifts/rotates the bottom colored squares
2. MOUSE selects a bottom square (adds white border)
3. The goal is probably to match the bottom squares to the top frames
Top frames (left to right): blue(b), green(N), yellow(Y), purple(p)
Bottom squares (left to right): green(N), purple(p), blue(b), yellow(Y)
I need to figure out what happens after selecting a square. Maybe I need to click on a top frame to "place" the selected color there? Or maybe I need to SPACE until the bottom matches the top order.
Let me check what changed after the mouse click, and then try clicking on one of the top frames.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after MOUSE click
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation

# Compare nodes
before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before['nodes']}
after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after['nodes']}

print("Before nodes:", len(before['nodes']), "After nodes:", len(after['nodes']))

# Check timer
print("Timer (node 20) before:", before_nodes.get(20, {}).get('pixels'))
print("Timer (node 20) after:", after_nodes.get(20, {}).get('pixels'))

# Check bottom squares for changes (look for white border)
for n in after['nodes']:
    if n['id'] not in before_nodes:
        print(f"New node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check if any node changed boundary or pixels
for n in after['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in before_nodes:
        bn = before_nodes[n['id']]
        if n['boundary'] != bn['boundary'] or n['pixels'] != bn['pixels']:
            print(f"Node {n['id']} changed: pixels {bn['pixels']}->{n['pixels']}, color {bn['color']}->{n['color']}")

# The purple square (node 24) was clicked - check if it got a white border
# Let's look at nodes near the purple square position
print("\nBottom area nodes:")
for n in after['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 20 and n['boundary'][0][0] >= 50:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
